shallowRef: Another Way to Declare Reactivity Besides ref
As a Vue developer, you probably know
well — but there's another reactive API for declaring state called
. What's the difference between the two? Let's take a look.
is a Vue 3 Composition API reactivity primitive. It lets you declare reactive state — when the value changes, Vue re-renders the related components.

is commonly used for nested reactive objects too:
See the Pen ref vs shallowRef - 1 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
by recursion — no matter how nested your object is, any change triggers a re-render:
See the Pen ref vs shallowRef - 2 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
No matter how deep you go, Vue re-renders on any change. In some cases this is unnecessary or wasteful — for example, when you just want to hold a reference to a DOM element:

<script setup>
  import { ref } from 'vue';

  const dom = ref(document.querySelector('h1'));
</script>
You only need to grab the DOM element — you don't need Vue deeply watching every property inside it. The deep watching costs perf for no real benefit.
is also a Vue 3 Composition API reactivity primitive, but it only watches the top level. Vue only re-renders when the top-level value changes.
What does that mean? Let's revisit the deeply-nested example:
See the Pen ref vs shallowRef - 3 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
This time, clicking the button doesn't re-render — that's
's behaviour. Only top-level changes trigger updates.
But what counts as "top level" is a bit subtle. Let's break it down.

bug. It's not. The "top level" is actually
itself — so to trigger an update, you need to replace the whole thing:
